shortDefinition: ML-KEM is NIST's standardized post-quantum key-encapsulation mechanism (FIPS 203), used to establish shared secrets that resist quantum attack.
definition: ML-KEM (Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism) is the post-quantum key-establishment algorithm standardized by NIST in FIPS 203, finalized on August 13, 2024. It replaces quantum-vulnerable key-exchange mechanisms such as RSA key transport and elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman in protocols like TLS, SSH, and VPNs.

As a key-encapsulation mechanism, ML-KEM lets two parties agree on a shared secret over an untrusted network: one party encapsulates a secret against the other's public key, and the recipient decapsulates it with their private key. The shared secret then keys fast symmetric encryption such as AES-256.

Its security rests on the Module Learning-with-Errors (Module-LWE) problem over structured lattices, a problem with no known efficient solution on either classical or quantum computers. That is what makes ML-KEM safe against Shor's algorithm, which breaks RSA and elliptic-curve cryptography.

sections.heading: Parameter sets and security levels
paragraphs: FIPS 203 defines three parameter sets. ML-KEM-512 targets NIST security category 1 (comparable to AES-128), ML-KEM-768 targets category 3 (AES-192), and ML-KEM-1024 targets category 5 (AES-256). ML-KEM-768 is the default choice in most deployments and the parameter set used in the TLS hybrid groups shipped by major browsers.

Keys and ciphertexts are larger than their elliptic-curve counterparts (kilobytes rather than tens of bytes), but ML-KEM's arithmetic is fast, and in practice handshake latency is dominated by the network, not the math.
bullets: ML-KEM-512: category 1, encapsulation key 800 B, ciphertext 768 B
ML-KEM-768: category 3, encapsulation key 1,184 B, ciphertext 1,088 B
ML-KEM-1024: category 5, encapsulation key 1,568 B, ciphertext 1,568 B
All parameter sets produce a 32-byte shared secret

sections.heading: Where ML-KEM is deployed today
paragraphs: ML-KEM is already the most widely deployed post-quantum algorithm. TLS 1.3 uses it in the hybrid group X25519MLKEM768, enabled by default in Chrome and Firefox; large CDNs and cloud providers terminate a growing share of all TLS traffic with it. OpenSSH added an mlkem768x25519 hybrid key exchange in version 9.9, and messaging protocols such as Signal's PQXDH and Apple's iMessage PQ3 use ML-KEM-style lattice KEMs to protect message keys.

Nearly all early deployments are hybrid: the session secret is derived from both an elliptic-curve exchange and an ML-KEM encapsulation, so breaking the session requires breaking both. Hybrids protect against harvest-now-decrypt-later collection while the new algorithm accumulates confidence.

sections.heading: From CRYSTALS-Kyber to ML-KEM
paragraphs: ML-KEM began as CRYSTALS-Kyber, the key-encapsulation winner of NIST's post-quantum standardization project announced in July 2022. During standardization NIST renamed it ML-KEM and made small technical changes (adjustments to the Fujisaki-Okamoto transform and added input validation), so 'Kyber' implementations are not byte-compatible with final FIPS 203. New systems should implement and advertise ML-KEM, treating Kyber as a historical name.

sections.heading: Migration considerations
paragraphs: Moving to ML-KEM starts with knowing where quantum-vulnerable key establishment happens: TLS terminations, VPN concentrators, SSH fleets, service meshes, and any custom protocol doing RSA or ECDH key exchange. From there, most organizations adopt hybrid groups first; they are backward-deployable and already supported by mainstream stacks.

Because public keys and ciphertexts grow to roughly a kilobyte, constrained environments (embedded devices, UDP-based protocols sensitive to packet size, HSM-backed systems) need testing rather than assumptions. Certificate chains and PKI are a separate work stream: signatures use ML-DSA or SLH-DSA, not ML-KEM.

faq.question: Is ML-KEM the same as Kyber?
faq.answer: ML-KEM is the finalized, standardized version of CRYSTALS-Kyber. NIST renamed the algorithm when publishing FIPS 203 in August 2024 and made small technical changes, so final ML-KEM is not interoperable with earlier Kyber draft implementations. Use ML-KEM as the current name and target FIPS 203 for new work.

faq.question: Is ML-KEM proven secure against quantum computers?
faq.answer: ML-KEM's security reduces to the Module-LWE lattice problem, which has withstood decades of classical and quantum cryptanalysis with no known efficient attack. Like all practical cryptography this is a well-studied hardness assumption, not an absolute proof, which is one reason deployments favor hybrid modes combining ML-KEM with elliptic-curve exchange.

faq.question: Which ML-KEM parameter set should I use?
faq.answer: ML-KEM-768 is the sensible default: it targets NIST security category 3, matches what browsers and TLS libraries ship in X25519MLKEM768, and its sizes are manageable. Use ML-KEM-1024 where policy requires category 5 (for example CNSA 2.0 environments), and avoid ML-KEM-512 unless a constrained system genuinely cannot afford the extra bytes.

faq.question: Does ML-KEM replace AES?
faq.answer: No. ML-KEM establishes the shared secret; AES (or another symmetric cipher) then encrypts the actual data. Symmetric algorithms like AES-256 are already considered quantum-safe at sufficient key lengths. It is the public-key exchange protecting those keys that quantum computers threaten, and that is the part ML-KEM replaces.

sources.title: NIST Releases First 3 Finalized Post-Quantum Encryption Standards

cryptiqAngle: ML-KEM is the destination for most key-exchange migrations, but you can only migrate the RSA and ECDH usage you know about. CryptiQ's cryptographic inventory finds quantum-vulnerable key establishment across your infrastructure (TLS configs, SSH fleets, VPNs, code), and its migration workflow moves each one to ML-KEM or a hybrid group with your team approving every change.
